deck.name: Advanced Idioms
deck.description: Sound like a native speaker by mastering these figurative and idiomatic expressions.

cards.definition: to avoid talking about the main topic directly
cards.context: Stop beating around the bush and just tell me what you really think.

cards.definition: something that happens very rarely
cards.context: I only see my cousins from Australia once in a blue moon.

cards.definition: to feel slightly ill or not well
cards.context: I'm feeling a bit under the weather today, so I think I'll stay home.

cards.definition: something that is very easy to do
cards.context: The exam was a piece of cake; I finished it in twenty minutes.

cards.term: to cost an arm and a leg
cards.definition: to be extremely expensive
cards.context: That new car must have cost him an arm and a leg.

cards.definition: to say or do something to make people feel more relaxed in a social situation
cards.context: A quick joke is a great way to break the ice at a party.

cards.definition: to decide to do something difficult or unpleasant that one has been avoiding
cards.context: I finally decided to bite the bullet and go to the dentist.

cards.term: to let the cat out of the bag
cards.definition: to accidentally reveal a secret
cards.context: I accidentally let the cat out of the bag about Sarah's surprise party.

cards.term: to be on the same page
cards.definition: to have the same understanding or agreement about something
cards.context: Before we start the project, let's make sure we're all on the same page.

cards.term: to hit the nail on the head
cards.definition: to describe exactly what is causing a situation or problem
cards.context: You really hit the nail on the head with that analysis.

cards.definition: a situation where you can enjoy the advantages of two very different things
cards.context: Living in the countryside but working in the city gives her the best of both worlds.

cards.definition: to be too late to take advantage of an opportunity
cards.context: I wanted to buy those tickets, but I waited too long and missed the boat.

cards.definition: to agree with someone completely
cards.context: My boss and I don't always see eye to eye on our marketing strategy.

cards.term: to kill two birds with one stone
cards.definition: to achieve two things at the same time with a single action
cards.context: By cycling to work, I'm getting exercise and saving money — killing two birds with one stone.

cards.definition: to make a bad situation even worse
cards.context: It rained all day, and then, to add insult to injury, the car wouldn't start.

cards.definition: to join a popular trend or activity
cards.context: Every brand is jumping on the AI bandwagon these days.

cards.definition: the last in a series of bad things that makes you lose your patience
cards.context: The car breaking down was the last straw; I'm selling it.

cards.definition: to do something to get rid of stress or strong emotions
cards.context: I like to go for a long run to blow off steam after a stressful day.

cards.definition: to do something in the easiest or cheapest way, often sacrificing quality
cards.context: They tried to cut corners to save money, and now the roof is leaking.

cards.definition: to set a limit or boundary beyond which you will not go
cards.context: I don't mind helping, but I draw the line at doing all your work for you.

cards.definition: to begin something earlier than others to get an advantage
cards.context: Starting the preparation today will give us a head start on the project.

cards.definition: to help someone with something
cards.context: Could you give me a hand with these heavy boxes?

cards.definition: to do more than what is expected or required
cards.context: Our customer service team always goes the extra mile to help our clients.

cards.definition: to watch someone or something carefully
cards.context: Can you keep an eye on my bag while I go to the bathroom?

cards.term: to make a long story short
cards.definition: used to skip details and get to the main point of a story
cards.context: Anyway, to make a long story short, we eventually found the keys.

cards.definition: to play a joke on someone by telling them something that isn't true
cards.context: Don't believe him; he's just pulling your leg!

cards.definition: to wait until the next day to make a decision
cards.context: That's a big decision; why don't you sleep on it and tell me tomorrow?

cards.term: to take something with a grain of salt
cards.definition: to not believe something completely because it might be exaggerated
cards.context: You should take everything he says with a grain of salt.

cards.term: the ball is in your court
cards.definition: it is your turn to take action or make a decision
cards.context: I've made my offer. Now the ball is in your court.

cards.definition: under all circumstances, no matter how difficult
cards.context: They have been best friends through thick and thin for over twenty years.

cards.definition: to reveal a secret (similar to 'let the cat out of the bag')
cards.context: He finally spilled the beans about where he was going for the weekend.

cards.term: to cut a long story short
cards.definition: used to end a story quickly by skipping the details
cards.context: Anyway, to cut a long story short, we got the job.

cards.definition: a major problem or issue that everyone is aware of but no one wants to talk about
cards.context: We need to talk about the budget; it's the elephant in the room.

cards.definition: to work or study until very late at night
cards.context: I had to burn the midnight oil to finish the report on time.

cards.term: to get a taste of your own medicine
cards.definition: to be treated in the same unpleasant way that you have treated others
cards.context: He's always making fun of people, but today he got a taste of his own medicine.

cards.term: to see the light at the end of the tunnel
cards.definition: to see the end of a long and difficult situation
cards.context: It's been a tough year, but I'm finally starting to see the light at the end of the tunnel.

cards.definition: to waste time being upset about something that has already happened and cannot be changed
cards.context: I know you're disappointed about the lost game, but there's no use crying over spilled milk.

cards.definition: to go to bed
cards.context: I'm exhausted; I think I'm going to hit the sack.

cards.definition: to avoid restarting a conflict or talking about a problem from the past
cards.context: I thought about bringing up the old argument, but I decided to let sleeping dogs lie.

cards.definition: to try to stay positive and brave in a difficult situation
cards.context: Keep your chin up! Things will get better soon.

cards.definition: suffering or hard work is necessary to achieve success
cards.context: It was a tough workout, but no pain, no gain!

cards.definition: said when the person you have just been talking about appears
cards.context: Hi Sarah! Speak of the devil, we were just talking about you.

cards.term: the best thing since sliced bread
cards.definition: a very good invention or idea
cards.context: I think this new app is the best thing since sliced bread.

cards.definition: to politely decline an offer now but suggest doing it later
cards.context: I can't make it to dinner tonight, but can I take a rain check?

cards.definition: to give up or surrender
cards.context: After trying for hours to fix the computer, I finally threw in the towel.
